text: Adding 10 sessions of speed endurance training (5-10 × 30-sec maximal running) over 40 days while reducing training volume by 36% improves 10-km performance by 3.2% in moderately trained runners.
Speed endurance training with reduced volume improves running economy by approximately 2% at both 60% vVO2max and 10-km race pace without changes in VO2max.
Speed endurance training increases velocity at VO2max (vVO2max) by 2.0% even when absolute VO2max remains unchanged, indicating improved running efficiency rather than aerobic capacity gains.
Running economy improvements from speed endurance training are abolished under slow-twitch fiber glycogen-depleted conditions, suggesting the gains depend on adaptations in slow-twitch muscle fibers.
Short maximal sprints (30-second repeats) can serve as a potent stimulus for improving distance running performance even when overall training volume is substantially reduced.
Speed endurance training reduces UCP3 expression in slow-twitch fibers and SERCA1 in fast-twitch fibers, lowering energy-consuming processes and contributing to improved running economy.
